_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
clinical_indications: Surgery is indicated for all patients with TGA except in those with irreversible pulmonary vascular disease.
TGA with intact ventricular septum presenting soon after birth: ASO is the best option (Class I).

TGA with intact ventricular septum presenting beyond 3–4 weeks of life with regressed left ventricle, between 1 and 2 months of age: ASO; extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO) support may be required in some cases (Class Ila).
TGA with a large VSD and/or a large PDA: ASO with VSD and/or PDA closure by 6 weeks of age (Class I).
TGA with VSD and CoA: ASO with VSD closure and arch repair as soon as possible (Class I). It is preferable to repair all lesions in a single stage.

TGA with VSD and significant left ventricular outflow obstruction (Class I): Sub-valvular pulmonary obstruction with normal or near-normal pulmonary valve and pulmonary annulus: ASO with resection of sub-valvular stenosis.

clinical_thresholds.note: For TGA with intact ventricular septum (surgery indicated earlier if baby is unstable or has associated persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn).
For TGA with a large VSD and/or a large PDA (to prevent early pulmonary vascular disease).
For TGA with intact ventricular septum presenting beyond 3–4 weeks of life with regressed left ventricle.

claim.label: Post procedure stills of ECHO with report

ppd_preauth.q: Clinical notes contain detailed history, signs & symptoms, and indication for procedure?
Echo/Doppler report is suggestive of Transposition of Great Arteries?

cpd_claim.q: Post-procedure ECHO still shows repair of the defect?
Detailed Discharge Summary mentioning date of follow-up submitted?

common_queries: Echo/Doppler report is not suggestive of Transposition of Great Arteries or is missing at pre-authorization.
Clinical notes are missing detailed history, signs & symptoms, or indication for procedure at pre-authorization.
Indoor case papers are not submitted at claim stage.
Detailed Procedure / Operative notes are not submitted at claim stage.
Post-procedure ECHO stills with report are not submitted or do not show repair of the defect at claim stage.
Detailed Discharge Summary is not submitted or does not mention the date of follow-up at claim stage.

clinical_key_pointers: Transposition of great arteries (TGA) is the most common cyanotic CHD at birth, accounting for approximately 5% of all CHDs.
In 70% of cases, there is no associated defect apart from ASD, PDA, or insignificant VSD; these cases are labeled as having simple TGA.
Association of TGA with other defects such as large VSD and left ventricular outflow tract obstruction or coarctation (CoA) is referred to as complex TGA.
It is a serious disease and most patients with TGA present very early in life, within few days or weeks after birth.
The average life expectancy for an untreated newborn is 0.65 years, with mortality rate at 1 year being close to 90%.
With the advent of improved surgical techniques and postoperative care, long-term survival is > 90% with very low reintervention rates.
The best surgical option for patients with TGA is an arterial switch operation (ASO) where both arteries are switched so that ventriculoarterial concordance is achieved.
ASO should be performed early, preferably within 3–4 weeks of life, before the left ventricular mass and volume regresses secondary to fall of PVR after birth.
surgical_options: Atrial switch operation (Senning or Mustard operation)
Systemic-to-pulmonary shunt followed by Rastelli-type repair or root translocation
additional_information.referral_criteria: Referral of cases to the appropriate level of care (as required) for treatment of patients under PMJAY.
